verses.text: Job swears by the living God that, while breath remains, his lips will not speak wickedness nor surrender his integrity. He holds fast his innocence under oath. A clear conscience before God is worth defending to the last breath.

Job swears by the living God that, while breath remains, his lips will not speak wickedness nor surrender his integrity. He holds fast his innocence under oath. A clear conscience before God is worth defending to the last breath.

Job swears by the living God that, while breath remains, his lips will not speak wickedness nor surrender his integrity. He holds fast his innocence under oath. A clear conscience before God is worth defending to the last breath.

Job swears by the living God that, while breath remains, his lips will not speak wickedness nor surrender his integrity. He holds fast his innocence under oath. A clear conscience before God is worth defending to the last breath.

'God forbid that I should justify you,' he tells the friends; his heart will not reproach him as long as he lives. He refuses to confess sins he has not done. To admit false guilt merely to please accusers would be to lie against the truth.

'God forbid that I should justify you,' he tells the friends; his heart will not reproach him as long as he lives. He refuses to confess sins he has not done. To admit false guilt merely to please accusers would be to lie against the truth.

'God forbid that I should justify you,' he tells the friends; his heart will not reproach him as long as he lives. He refuses to confess sins he has not done. To admit false guilt merely to please accusers would be to lie against the truth.

Yet Job is no hypocrite, for he asks, 'what is the hope of the hypocrite... when God taketh away his soul?' Will God hear his cry? The hollow man has no refuge in the day of death. Hope that fails at the grave was never real hope.

Yet Job is no hypocrite, for he asks, 'what is the hope of the hypocrite... when God taketh away his soul?' Will God hear his cry? The hollow man has no refuge in the day of death. Hope that fails at the grave was never real hope.

Job offers to teach them 'by the hand of God,' marvelling that men who have seen so much remain so vain. He knows God's ways better than his judges. Long experience of God should produce humility, not empty argument.

Job offers to teach them 'by the hand of God,' marvelling that men who have seen so much remain so vain. He knows God's ways better than his judges. Long experience of God should produce humility, not empty argument.
He grants the wicked's true portion: his children fall by sword or famine, and his widows do not even weep. Judgment overtakes his house. The ungodly leave behind not blessing but sorrow and loss.

He grants the wicked's true portion: his children fall by sword or famine, and his widows do not even weep. Judgment overtakes his house. The ungodly leave behind not blessing but sorrow and loss.

Though he heaps up silver like dust, the just will wear it; he lies down rich one night and wakes to find it gone. His wealth slips through his fingers. What is gathered without God cannot be securely kept.

Terrors sweep over him like a flood, the storm-wind carries him off in the night, and men hiss and clap at his ruin. His end is sudden and despised. The wicked's seeming security is swept away in a moment.
